Description: The fundamental pillars that define how users interact with and perceive digital interfaces.

Principles.Title: User-Centered Design
Principles.Effect: Prioritizing user needs and behaviors above all else to ensure the system solves real problems.

Principles.Title: Consistency & Standards
Principles.Effect: Using familiar patterns so users don't have to learn new behaviors for common tasks.

Principles.Title: Clarity & Simplicity
Principles.Effect: Removing unnecessary elements to reduce cognitive load and focus attention on primary tasks.

Description: Psychological mandates that govern how human beings process information and navigate movement.

Principles.Title: Hick's Law
Principles.Effect: Decision time increases with the number of choices. Simplify options to speed up user flow.

Principles.Title: Fitts's Law
Principles.Effect: Interactive targets should be large enough and close enough to be easily acquired.
BestPractices: Place primary actions near thumb zones

Principles.Title: Jakob's Law
Principles.Effect: Users spend most of their time on other sites; they prefer yours to work the same way.

Description: Rules describing how the human brain automatically groups similar or local elements together.

Principles.Title: Law of Proximity
Principles.Effect: Elements close to each other are perceived as a related group or a single unit.
BestPractices: Use tight spacing for labels and inputs
Group related cards in a container

Principles.Title: Law of Similarity
Principles.Effect: Elements with similar visual traits (color, shape, size) are perceived as related.
BestPractices: Define clear primary vs secondary button styles
Color-code states like Success and Error

Principles.Title: Law of Continuity
Principles.Effect: The eye is drawn to follow paths, lines, and curves, even if they are broken.
BestPractices: Align content along a common axis
Ensure seamless flow in multi-step forms

Description: Ensuring the interface is usable and understandable for everyone, regardless of ability or context.

Principles.Title: Contrast & Readability
Principles.Effect: Ensuring text is legible against any background for users with visual impairments.
BestPractices: Avoid thin fonts on light backgrounds

Principles.Title: Inclusive Interaction
Principles.Effect: Providing multiple ways to interact, such as keyboard navigation and screen reader support.

Principles.Title: Error Prevention & Recovery
Principles.Effect: Designing to prevent mistakes and providing clear ways to 'undo' or fix them when they happen.
BestPractices: Use confirmation dialogs for destructive actions
Provide clear error messages with solutions

Description: The strategic use of visual weight to guide the user's eye and control the reading order.

Principles.Title: Size & Scale
Principles.Effect: Large elements command more attention; use them for the most important messages.
BestPractices: Make primary CTA larger than secondary

Principles.Title: Contrast as Priority
Principles.Effect: The most important element should be the most visually distinct from its surroundings.
BestPractices: Use vibrant colors for main actions
Use white space as a focal tool

Principles.Title: Scanning Patterns (F & Z)
Principles.Effect: Designing for the natural way humans scan screens (F-pattern for text, Z-pattern for ads/landings).
BestPractices: Put CTAs at the end of scan paths
Use bullet points for quick scannability

Description: Based on Nielsen's heuristics, these are broad rules of thumb for interface usability.

Principles.Title: User Control & Freedom
Principles.Effect: Users often perform actions by mistake and need a clearly marked 'emergency exit' without an extended dialogue.

Principles.Title: Recognition over Recall
Principles.Effect: Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible.

Principles.Title: Help & Documentation
Principles.Effect: While better if the system is used without it, it may be necessary to provide documentation focused on user tasks.

Description: Scientific theories on how human memory and attention spans dictate the success of an interface.

Principles.Title: Miller’s Law
Principles.Effect: The average person can only keep 7 (± 2) items in their working memory. Grouping is essential.
BestPractices: Break long forms into small sections

Principles.Title: Peak-End Rule
Principles.Effect: Users judge an experience largely based on how they felt at its peak and at its end.
BestPractices: Ensure the exit flow is smooth

Principles.Title: Zeigarnik Effect
Principles.Effect: People remember unfinished or interrupted tasks better than completed ones.
BestPractices: Use progress bars to motivate completion
Provide 'resume where you left off' features

Description: Methods to manage complexity and maximize the impact of design efforts.

Principles.Title: Tesler’s Law
Principles.Effect: The Law of Conservation of Complexity: every application has an inherent amount of complexity that cannot be removed.
BestPractices: Handle complexity on the backend, not the UI

Principles.Title: Pareto Principle
Principles.Effect: The 80/20 Rule: 80% of users will only use 20% of your features. Focus your design on those.

Principles.Title: Doherty Threshold
Principles.Effect: Productivity skyrockets when interaction feedback occurs in less than 400ms.
BestPractices: Use skeleton screens for perceived speed

Description: Building trust and delight while avoiding manipulative 'Dark Patterns'.

Principles.Title: Aesthetic-Usability Effect
Principles.Effect: Users often perceive aesthetically pleasing design as more usable.
BestPractices: Use a cohesive, modern color palette

Principles.Title: Ethical Design (Anti-Dark Patterns)
Principles.Effect: Ensuring users aren't tricked into actions (like subscriptions) through deceptive UI.

Principles.Title: Visceral Interaction
Principles.Effect: Appealing to the user's immediate emotional reaction through sensory triggers.
